skill.name: doc-coauthoring
skill.description: Guides users through a structured workflow for drafting, refining, and reader-testing documentation, proposals, technical specs, and decision documents.
skill.summary: Co-author structured documents through context gathering, section drafting, iterative refinement, and reader testing.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, and network patterns, but the shell and crypto alerts are false positives from Markdown workflow text and tool names. The remaining real concerns are legitimate workflow behaviors: optional connector access to shared documents or channels, and creating or editing documentation files.

medium_findings.title: Optional Connector Access to Shared Documents and Channels
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to use available integrations to fetch shared documents or read messaging context. This is a legitimate documentation workflow, but it can expose sensitive organizational content if users grant broad connector access without reviewing scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly tells the assistant to read shared documents or use integrations when available. The behavior is user-directed and not malicious, but it creates a real privacy and access-control concern.

medium_findings.title: User Workspace File Creation and Editing
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the assistant to create Markdown files and use artifact editing tools while drafting documents. This is expected for co-authoring, but it requires normal workspace write permissions and user review before important files are overwritten or changed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly call for creating files and replacing document text. The purpose is legitimate, but the file-write behavior is concrete and should be disclosed.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections occur inside Markdown inline code references such as create_file and str_replace, plus prose about creating Markdown files. No shell command, Ruby execution, or command interpolation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is Markdown documentation naming editor tools or files, not executable Ruby or shell code. There is no evidence of command execution syntax beyond inline-code formatting.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain ordinary documentation words such as doc, docs, Claude, and workflow text. No hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic API usage is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic operation at the listed locations. The alerts appear to be keyword matches inside natural-language documentation.

low_findings.title: Static Network Reconnaissance Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The flagged line is an instruction for the user to provide an information dump or link shared documents. It does not describe scanning hosts, discovering services, or probing networks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is plain workflow guidance for collecting user-provided context. No network reconnaissance command or probing behavior appears in the surrounding text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Claude URL Is Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points users to Claude.ai for manual reader testing. It is not used for automatic data transfer, credential collection, or hidden outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is shown as a user-facing instruction to open a fresh Claude conversation. There is no code path that sends data to the URL automatically.

content.user_title: Draft Better Documentation with Guided Review
content.value_statement: Complex documents often fail because context, structure, and reader assumptions are unclear. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a repeatable co-authoring workflow that gathers context, drafts sections, and tests reader understanding.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through context gathering before drafting begins.
Suggests document structures for specs, proposals, RFCs, and decision documents.
Builds sections through clarifying questions, brainstorming, curation, drafting, and refinement.
Creates an artifact or local Markdown scaffold when the environment supports file creation.
Uses fresh-context reader testing to identify ambiguity, missing context, and contradictions.
Provides final review prompts for facts, links, reader impact, and document ownership.
limitations: Connector access depends on the host environment and user permissions.
It does not independently verify facts, links, or technical claims.
The workflow can be slower than freeform drafting for short documents.
Local file edits require user review when existing files may be affected.

use_cases.title: Draft a Technical Design Spec
use_cases.description: Turn scattered architecture context into a structured spec with clear trade-offs, open questions, and review-ready sections.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Product Proposal
use_cases.description: Shape goals, audience needs, constraints, and expected impact into a proposal that stakeholders can evaluate quickly.

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing Decision Document
use_cases.description: Review a draft for missing context, unclear assumptions, contradictions, and reader questions before sharing it widely.

prompt_templates.title: Start a New Doc
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for starting the guided workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me co-author a technical document. Start by asking the Stage 1 context questions and wait for my answers before drafting.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Document Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt when the user knows the document type.
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a decision document for this project. Use the co-authoring workflow to gather context, then suggest 3 to 5 sections before creating a scaffold.

prompt_templates.title: Refine One Section
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the section refinement process on the technical approach section. Ask clarifying questions, brainstorm candidate points, then draft only after I choose what to keep.

prompt_templates.title: Run Reader Testing
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for validating a near-final document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the reader testing stage on this draft. Predict realistic reader questions, test for ambiguity and missing context, then recommend targeted fixes.

output_examples.input: I need to write an RFC about replacing our deployment system.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for audience, desired impact, constraints, current pain points, alternatives, timeline, and stakeholder concerns before proposing an RFC structure.

output_examples.input: Review this decision doc before I send it to leadership.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks flow, redundancy, assumptions, reader questions, unclear trade-offs, and contradictions, then proposes specific edits by section.

output_examples.input: Test whether new engineers can understand this onboarding doc.
output_examples.output: The assistant generates reader questions, simulates fresh-context answers when possible, identifies missing background, and recommends focused clarifications.

best_practices: Provide audience, desired impact, constraints, and known disagreements before drafting starts.
Review file edits and connector-sourced context before relying on generated sections.
Use reader testing on important documents before sharing them with a wider audience.
anti_patterns: Skipping context gathering and asking for a polished final document immediately.
Granting broad connector access when pasted excerpts would be enough.
Treating reader testing as fact verification instead of checking clarity and assumptions.

faq.question: What kinds of documents does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports technical specs, proposals, RFCs, decision documents, PRDs, and other structured workplace documents.

faq.question: Does it require external connectors?
faq.answer: No. Connectors can help gather context, but users can paste relevant content directly instead.

faq.question: Can it edit local files?
faq.answer: Yes, when artifacts are unavailable it may create or edit Markdown files in the working directory.

faq.question: Does it verify whether document claims are true?
faq.answer: No. It helps with structure, clarity, and reader understanding, but users must verify facts and links.

faq.question: Why does the workflow use reader testing?
faq.answer: Reader testing finds missing context, ambiguity, and assumptions that authors may not notice.

faq.question: Is this useful for short notes?
faq.answer: It can help, but the full workflow is best for documents where clarity and review quality matter.
